  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "What does the term 'e-waste' primarily refer to?",
    "options": ["Discarded electronic devices and equipment", "Natural waste from earthquakes", "Biodegradable food waste", "Industrial chemical byproducts"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "E-waste, or electronic waste, specifically refers to discarded electronic and electrical devices, such as old computers, phones, and televisions."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "Which of the following is a primary environmental concern associated with the improper disposal of e-waste in landfills?",
    "options": ["Leaching of heavy metals and toxic substances into soil and groundwater", "Depletion of the ozone layer", "Increased frequency of acid rain", "Rise in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": E-waste contains toxic heavy metals like lead, mercury, and cadmium. When placed in landfills, these substances can leach out, contaminating the surrounding soil and groundwater."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "The 'urban mining' approach to e-waste management is best described as:",
    "options": ["Recovering valuable materials like gold and copper from discarded electronics", "Extracting minerals from landfill sites using traditional mining techniques", "Using genetically modified bacteria to consume electronic components", "Building landfills within city limits to reduce transportation emissions"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "Urban mining is the process of reclaiming valuable compounds and elements (precious metals, rare earth elements, copper, etc.) from existing electronic products and waste, treating e-waste as a resource rather than just trash."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "Which international agreement specifically aims to protect human health and the environment from the harmful effects of hazardous wastes, including e-waste, by controlling their transboundary movements?",
    "options": ["The Basel Convention", "The Kyoto Protocol", "The Montreal Protocol", "The Paris Agreement"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The Basel Convention on the Control of Transboundary Movements of Hazardous Wastes and their Disposal is the key international treaty that regulates the global movement of hazardous waste, including e-waste, to prevent its dumping in developing countries."
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "A major challenge in implementing a formal, closed-loop e-waste recycling system, compared to an informal, linear one, is:",
    "options": ["The high capital and operational cost of establishing safe and efficient processing facilities", "The lack of valuable materials in modern electronic devices", "The inability of formal systems to effectively collect e-waste from consumers", "Formal systems producing more toxic byproducts than informal ones"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "Formal recycling requires significant investment in technology, infrastructure, and skilled labor to safely dismantle products, recover materials, and treat hazardous components, making it more expensive to operate than informal, often unsafe, backyard recycling operations."
